content.claim: Free parental school choice combined with catchment-area advantage rules drives educational segregation more strongly than residential segregation patterns; towns with higher school-choice-driven student mobility show stronger inter-school inequalities, and local educational policies have weaker segregationist effects than choice mechanics.
content.context: Fresh live research through the Portuguese system reform lens
limitations: Mechanism is demonstrated in Hungarian context but generalizability to other systems unclear
Interaction between choice, catchment rules, and gimnázium selection is not fully isolated
Local policy heterogeneity is substantial, limiting predictive power
content.population: Population described in the cited finding
content.time_scope: As reported by the cited source
